exemplar.title: The relationship of the mass ratio on post-collision velocities in elastic collisions

detailedExemplarData.title: The relationship of the mass ratio on post-collision velocities in elastic collisions

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are both clear and precise, with units, uncertainties and sample calculations clearly shown.
Uncertainty propagation and gradientâuncertainty calculations are methodical and correctly reference standard formulas.
Processing steps (averaging, correlation) align accurately with theory and are presented consistently.
weaknesses: Some raw-data tables contain empty cells, creating minor confusion.
Uncertainty sources rely on reactionâtime estimates rather than sensor resolution, leading to significant omissions.
Determination of distance uncertainty (Â±0.001 m) is not explained through calibration or instrument limits.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With strand scores of 5 (communication), 4 (uncertainties) and 5 (processing quality), the work falls solidly in the 5-6 band. Minor omissions in uncertainty justification prevent a perfect score, warranting a 5.

strengths: The research question is situated within a clear, disciplineâspecific context with reference to conservation laws and realâworld applications.
The stepâbyâstep method, annotated diagram and materials list largely allow for reproducibility.
A comprehensive risk assessment and identification of control variables demonstrate robust methodological planning.
weaknesses: Exact positions for motionâsensor placement and the measured spring displacement value are not specified, reducing experimental reproducibility.
Explanations for why nine mass ratios and three trials ensure sufficient data are thin.
Potential energy losses (e.g. sound, deformation) are not acknowledged in the theoretical context.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores of 5 (context), 4 (data-collection methodology) and 3 (methodological considerations) average to the mid-3-4 band. While context is excellent, omissions in replicability detail and depth of methodological explanation cap the criterion at 4.

strengths: The conclusion explicitly references Pearson coefficients, p-values and qualitative agreement with theoretical expectations.
Key findings are restated in the context of conservation laws, demonstrating conceptual understanding.
weaknesses: No quantitative comparison between measured and predicted velocities is provided (no residual analysis).
The strength of agreement with theoretical predictions is not numerically evaluated or discussed.
Scientific context comparison lacks depth and explicit linkage to high-school physics conventions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With one strand at the upper end of band 3-4 and the other at the lower end, the overall performance aligns with the 3-4 descriptor but leans toward the 3 level due to missing quantitative justification.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (air resistance, friction, sensor precision, sample size) are identified and linked to energy losses or statistical power.
Realistic and relevant improvements (vacuum chamber, track maintenance, sensor calibration, increased sample size) are described.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each methodological weakness (e.g., percentage energy loss) is not quantified.
Improvements are not explained in terms of how much they would enhance accuracy or reliability.
The suggestion to conduct a power analysis is not fully elaborated.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands meet the âdescribedâ level (band 3-4) but lack the depth of explanation required for band 5-6. A midpoint score of 3 reflects clear identification and description without relativeâimpact explanation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student formulates a focused research question and embeds it in a clear, discipline-specific context: elastic collisions on a horizontal track, with explicit reference to conservation laws and everyday applications (billiards, crash testing).  The introduction explains why mass ratio is the key variable and links it directly to measurable post-collision velocities, thus providing the necessary specificity expected at the top band.  Minor wording slips do not detract from the contextual clarity, so the strand sits comfortably in the 5-6 range; a mid-top score of 5 is appropriate.

Instrument uncertainties are stated; propagation is demonstrated for representative calculations; error bars and gradient uncertainties are derived.  However, time uncertainty is taken as reaction-time range rather than sensor resolution, and identical Â±0.01 m sâ»Â¹ is applied to all velocities without recalculation for each trial, indicating omissions.  This matches âsome significant omissions,â positioning the work at the top of the 3-4 band.

Methodological considerations are addressed through tables of variables, risk assessment and preliminary trials.  The student states why each control matters and describes some limitations.  Nonetheless, explanations of *sufficiency* (e.g., why only nine mass ratios or why three trials ensure reliability) are thin, and the influence of sensor resolution on data quality is not analysed.  The work therefore meets the âdescribedâ level but does not reach the depth of âexplained,â placing it at the lower end of the 3-4 band.

Specific weaknesses (air resistance, friction, sensor precision, sample size) are identified and briefly linked to energy loss or statistical power, indicating more than generic statements.  However, the explanation of *relative impact* (magnitude or direction of error) is minimal, so the work fits the âdescribesâ rather than âexplainsâ descriptor, earning 3 within the 3-4 band.

The step-by-step method, list of materials, and annotated diagram allow another investigator to replicate most of the data-collection procedure.  However, details such as how the spring launch produces a *measured* constant initial velocity, precise motion-sensor positions for *each* cart and the exact distance intervals are only partly described, leaving some ambiguity.  These omissions prevent full reproducibility yet still satisfy the descriptor for âfew ambiguities,â warranting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Averaging, uncertainty propagation, velocity calculations and correlation statistics are executed correctly and consistently with physics principles.  The processed velocities align with the conservation equations, and graphs include best-fit lines produced via software.  No major computational mistakes are evident, so the processing is accurate and appropriate, satisfying the 5-6 descriptor (score 5).

Realistic improvements (vacuum chamber, track cleaning, sensor calibration, larger sample size) are offered and linked to each weakness, fulfilling the âdescribedâ level.  Yet the student does not explain *how much* each suggestion would enhance accuracy or reliability, so the strand stays in the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

Raw-data and processed-data tables include units, uncertainties, and clear headings; sample calculations are shown and correctly referenced; graphs are labelled with axes and legends.  Overall presentation is both clear (readable structure) and precise (consistent significant figures, correct units).  Minor formatting issues (some empty column cells) are negligible, so the strand merits the 5-6 band, awarded 5.

The student links results to conservation laws and quotes theory qualitatively, but does not present calculated theoretical velocity values for each mass ratio nor compare residuals.  The comparison is therefore present but only partially developed, matching the lower end of the 3-4 band.

The conclusion refers explicitly to Pearson coefficients, p-values and qualitative agreement with theoretical expectations, thus tying findings to the analysis.  Yet numerical comparison with *predicted* velocities from equations (1) and (2) is absent, and the strength of agreement is not quantified.  Hence the conclusion is âdescribed but not fully consistent,â placing it in the upper 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The introduction embeds the research question within a clear, disciplineâspecific context, referencing conservation laws and realâworld applications effectively.
The definition of inelastic collisions is incorrect: it refers to collisions conserving kinetic energy, which should be classified as elastic. This misdefinition could lead to conceptual confusion.
Consider outlining in the introduction how the initial velocity will be measured and controlled to improve clarity on experimental reproducibility.
The conservation of momentum equation is presented clearly and correctly, laying a solid theoretical foundation for the analysis.
It would strengthen the theory section to acknowledge potential energy losses in realistic collisions and their sources, such as sound or deformation.
The identification of independent and dependent variables with apparatus and uncertainties is comprehensive and wellâstructured, aiding reproducibility.
Refine the alternative hypothesis to specify the expected positive linear relationship, which will guide statistical analysis more explicitly.
Specify exact positions or distances for motion sensor placement to reduce ambiguity and ensure consistency across trials.
Provide the measured spring displacement value rather than 'maximum displacement' to enable precise replication of initial velocity conditions.
The preliminary trials demonstrate iterative refinement by replacing stopwatches with motion sensors, effectively reducing random timing errors.
The risk assessment covers safety, ethical, and environmental considerations thoroughly, demonstrating robust methodological planning.
Link qualitative observations directly to the quantitative analysis by noting specific trends or anomalies observed during collisions.
Table 4 on raw data is left blank; ensure raw data tables are completed or removed to avoid confusion in data reporting.
Clarify how the distance uncertainty of Â±0.001 m was determined, for example by specifying calibration or measurement limits on the ruler.
Sample calculation of average times is clearly shown and provides a transparent template for processing similar data.
Using (maxâmin)/2 as uncertainty ignores instrument resolution; consider propagating the digital sensorâs specified uncertainty instead.
Uncertainty propagation for initial velocity incorporates both distance and time uncertainties correctly following standard errorâpropagation formula.
Include a brief note on how theoretical equations (1) and (2) will be validated against these processed velocity values for consistency.
Calculation of theoretical vâ using derived equations is accurate and clearly shown, reinforcing the link between theory and data.
Discuss if the reactionâtime method used in preliminary trials could introduce bias in uncertainty estimates compared with sensor resolution data.
Report numerical values for Îd and Ît used in this calculation for transparency in uncertainty propagation.
Consider converting the mass ratio categories into numeric massâratio values (mâ/mâ) on the x-axis for linear regression validity.
Interpret the p-values in terms of correlation strength and discuss whether the moderate significance for Car 2 warrants further investigation.
Detail the method used to approximate standard deviation for gradient uncertainties, including the number of sample points used.
Gradient uncertainty calculations for both data sets are presented methodically, demonstrating rigorous treatment of fit errors.
The conclusion restates observations but lacks quantitative comparison between measured velocities and theoretical predictions including residual analysis.
Quantify the relative impact of friction and air resistance on velocity (e.g., estimate percentage energy loss) to deepen the evaluation.
The suggestion to conduct a power analysis is truncated; elaborate how increasing sample size would statistically strengthen correlation findings.
The strengths table effectively highlights precise measurement, controlled variables, and standardized procedures supporting experimental rigor.
For proposed extensions, outline specific methods for measuring restitution coefficients or variable friction to facilitate experimental design.
Expand the bibliography by including peerâreviewed journal articles or textbooks to reinforce the scientific context of elastic collision theory.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
